bpm feed — conversation Claude

14 juillet 2026 · claude-fable-5 · 5 images
H
Hugo
/grill-with-docs
arguments
Your goal is to accomplish what is asked in @docs/brief.md 
We will use the latest stable version of Expo and React native.
Here are some constraints to correctly setup the project and will help you run the grilling session.

Follow this architecture:

```
src/
  app/                     # Expo Router. thin route files only
    _layout.tsx            # root: providers, fonts, splash, error boundary
    (auth)/
      login.tsx            # → export { LoginScreen as default } from '@/features/auth'
    (tabs)/
      _layout.tsx
      index.tsx
      profile.tsx
  features/                # domain modules. the actual app
    auth/
      components/
      hooks/
      api.ts               # this feature's queries/mutations
      store.ts             # zustand slice only if genuinely client state
      index.ts             # public surface of the feature
    posts/
      ...
  components/ui/           # shared primitives: Button, Text, Card
  lib/                     # api client, storage wrapper, analytics, zod env parsing
  hooks/                   # shared cross-feature hooks
  theme/                   # design tokens
```

You must use the data structure I provided you under the folder `.src/types/feed.ts` , do not create new interfaces or enums or do not duplicate them in the codebase.

I provide you a screen of the app which is a mockup to let you understand how the UI will look like.

For the fonts: the titles must use the “Cal Sans” font available under `src/assets/fonts/cal-sans-regular.ttf`.

For the colors: create a file under `src/utils/colors.ts` and use it everywhere when needed. Based on the screenshot and what is asked in the brief, please add some relevant colors in this file if needed. Do not put every some hardcode hexadecimal values inside pages if not relevant. 

You must follow these rules for file naming and files composition :

- Component — `post-card.tsx` — exports `function PostCard`, `type PostCardProps`
- Screen — `login-screen.tsx` — exports `function LoginScreen`
- Hook — `use-auth.ts` — exports `function useAuth`
- Store — `store.ts` (inside the feature) — exports `const useAuthStore`
- Queries/mutations — `api.ts` (inside the feature) — exports `usePost`, `postKeys`
- Zod schemas + types — `schemas.ts` — exports `postSchema`, `type Post`
- Constants — `constants.ts` — exports `MAX_RETRIES` (SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE)
- Test — `post-card.test.tsx` — co-located with the file it tests
- Route — `_layout.tsx`, `[id].tsx`, `(tabs)/` — names dictated by Expo Router

Use Stylesheet for styling, do not create common file for styling, just put the classes in const styles - Stylesheet.create({})

You must respect short files and split any code that can be relevant to split into components to make them reusable. Avoid any useless props for each component. add a prop to a component only when it is relevant and use during the implementation you are doing.



You’ll setup knip, biome, typecheck and vitest in an independant issue too.
✳

1	// Types for the profile feed API response.
2	// See docs/feed-response-example.json for a sample payload.
3	
4	// ============ Shared vocabularies ============
5	
6	export type Gender = 'woman' | 'man';
7	
8	export type TrainingFrequency = 'little' | 'mid' | 'hard';
9	
10	export type PromptCategory = 'lifestyle' | 'sport';
11	
12	export type RelationshipType = 'exclusive' | 'casual' | 'intimate';
13	
14	export type Zodiac =
15	  | 'aries'
16	  | 'taurus'
17	  | 'gemini'
18	  | 'cancer'
19	  | 'leo'
20	  | 'virgo'
21	  | 'libra'
22	  | 'scorpio'
23	  | 'sagittarius'
24	  | 'capricorn'
25	  | 'aquarius'
26	  | 'pisces';
27	
28	export type Diet =
29	  | 'omnivore'
30	  | 'flexitarian'
31	  | 'vegetarian'
32	  | 'pescatarian'
33	  | 'vegan';
34	
35	// Frequency-style habits: the sample payload only shows a subset of values,
36	// they likely share a fuller scale (never / sometimes / socially / often)
37	export type DrinkingHabit = 'never' | 'socially' | 'often';
38	
39	export type SmokingHabit = 'never' | 'sometimes';
40	
41	export type DrugsHabit = 'never';
42	
43	export type KidsIntention =
44	  | 'want'
45	  | 'dont_want'
46	  | 'unsure'
47	  | 'have'
48	  | 'have_want_more'
49	  | 'dont_have';
50	
51	export type PetsStatus = 'dog' | 'cat' | 'other' | 'none' | 'want';
52	
53	export type Religion =
54	  | 'agnostic'
55	  | 'atheist'
56	  | 'spiritual'
57	  | 'christian'
58	  | 'muslim'
59	  | 'buddhist'
60	  | 'other';
61	
62	// ============ Card contents ============
63	
64	export interface PictureContent {
65	  storageKey: string;
66	  imageUrl: string;
67	  thumbhash: string;
68	  promptKey: string | null; // a picture can optionally answer a prompt
69	  promptTitle: string | null;
70	}
71	
72	export interface PromptAnswerContent {
73	  promptKey: string;
74	  promptTitle: string;
75	  answerText: string;
76	  category: PromptCategory;
77	}
78	
79	export interface SportPractice {
80	  sportKey: string; // open vocabulary ("climbing", "padel", ...)
81	  sportName: string; // localized display name
82	  sportIcon: string; // icon identifier ("mountain", "bike", ...)
83	  trainingFrequency: TrainingFrequency;
84	}
85	
86	export interface SportCardContent {
87	  sports: SportPractice[];
88	  trainingFrequency: TrainingFrequency; // overall frequency across sports
89	}
90	
91	export interface InfoCardContent {
92	  age: number;
93	  height: number; // cm
94	  city: string;
95	  relationshipType: RelationshipType;
96	  zodiac: Zodiac;
97	  diet: Diet;
98	  drinking: DrinkingHabit;
99	  smoking: SmokingHabit;
100	  drugs: DrugsHabit;
101	  kids: KidsIntention;
102	  pets: PetsStatus;
103	  religion: Religion;
104	  job: string;
105	  education: string | null;
106	  originCity: string;
107	}
108	
109	// Blurred/teaser picture: no storageKey or imageUrl on purpose
110	export interface LockedPictureContent {
111	  thumbhash: string;
112	  promptTitle: string | null;
113	}
114	
115	// ============ Cards (discriminated union) ============
116	
117	interface BaseCard {
118	  id: string; // UUID
119	  position: number; // display order within the profile
120	}
121	
122	export interface PictureCard extends BaseCard {
123	  type: 'picture';
124	  content: PictureContent;
125	}
126	
127	export interface PromptAnswerCard extends BaseCard {
128	  type: 'prompt_answer';
129	  content: PromptAnswerContent;
130	}
131	
132	export interface SportCard extends BaseCard {
133	  type: 'sport_card';
134	  content: SportCardContent;
135	}
136

✳Réflexion

I've explored everything in the repo. Here's what I'm working with:

What exists: brief.md (bpm = dating app for athletes; recreate the feed UI + pass/like animations), feed.ts (a well-made discriminated union: picture, prompt_answer, sport_card, info_card, locked_picture cards under Profile → FeedResponse), feed-response-example.json (10 French profiles, 3–6 cards each), and the Cal Sans font. No Expo app, no package.json, not even a git repo yet — full blank slate.

Gaps I noticed already (we'll grill through these one by one): - The brief's vocabulary (photo, prompt, sport, stat) doesn't match the data's (picture, prompt_answer, sport_card, info_card) — and locked_picture exists in data but isn't mentioned in the brief at all. - The palette includes boost and superlike colors but the brief only asks for like and pass. - You said colors go in src/utils/colors.ts, but the architecture you gave has src/theme/ for design tokens — one of these has to win. - The brief's !image.png mockup is referenced but not in the repo.

That last one is the blocker, so it's my first question:

Q1 — Where is the UI mockup?

The whole case is "code the exact bpm feed (the same UI)," so nearly every UI decision (card layout, header, like/pass buttons, how info/sport cards render, what "locked" looks like) hangs on that screenshot. There's no image anywhere in the repo.

My recommendation: drop the screenshot into the repo as docs/mockup.png (versioned, and I can read it directly), or just paste the image into this chat. If you paste it here, I'll still suggest committing it to docs/ so future sessions can reference it.

Can you share it?

H
Hugo
✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

The mockup answers a lot. Here's what I extract from it:

  • Dark theme — #0D0D0D surface, cards on slightly lighter dark panels with rounded corners.
  • Header — profile firstname ("Inès") as a big Cal Sans title, top-left. Top-right: a pill with filters + undo/rewind icons, then a circular boost button (blue #48C1F3, lightning icon, badge "1").
  • Feed body — vertically scrolling cards: a big photo card, and below it a partially-visible sport card ("SESSIONS / WEEK", activity-rings graphic, sports listed with dotted leaders and values like "5+" in accent colors).
  • Like — white circular heart button sitting bottom-right on the card.
  • Pass — dark circular X button floating bottom-left, seemingly fixed over the feed.
  • Tab bar — Home (bpm logo), Likes (badge "4"), Matches, Profile.

Two things worth flagging: the mockup's sport card shows "5+" sessions/week while your data has trainingFrequency: 'little' | 'mid' | 'hard' (so there's a mapping to define), and the mockup text is English while your JSON content is French. I'll grill both later.

Also: I can't write a pasted chat image to disk — if you can, drop it at docs/mockup.png so it's versioned for future sessions.

Q2 — What's the feed's navigation model?

The mockup shows one profile's cards stacked vertically. Two readings:

A. Profile-sequential (Hinge-style): the screen shows one profile at a time — its cards in a vertical scroll, header = that profile's name. Like (on a card) or Pass (X) ends that profile: animation plays, feed advances to the next profile's cards, header updates. The undo button suggests this sequential flow.

B. Continuous interleaved feed (TikTok/Instagram-style): all profiles' cards in one endless scroll; the header name swaps as you cross profile boundaries; Pass removes the current profile's remaining cards from the scroll.

My recommendation: A. It matches the brief's mental model ("a profile has multiple cards", like a card / pass), makes the pass/like animations natural (whole-profile transition), makes undo trivially well-defined, and it's how bpm actually behaves as far as I can tell.

Which is it?

H
Hugo

A - yes, vertical scroll only. However, no endless scroll, each profile is display one at a time, the next profile is shown when the user send a like (each card is likable, whatever the type). Or the next profile is shown when the user hits the sticky left cross-button

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

A it is — and the "each card is likable, whatever the type" detail is exactly the kind of thing I wanted pinned down. Let me start the glossary now that we have our first resolved terms.

⛭2 actions · Read, Write
Read/Users/hugobayoud/.claude/skills/grill-with-docs/CONTEXT-FORMAT.md
1	# CONTEXT.md Format
2	
3	## Structure
4	
5	```md
6	# {Context Name}
7	
8	{One or two sentence description of what this context is and why it exists.}
9	
10	## Language
11	
12	**Order**:
13	{A one or two sentence description of the term}
14	_Avoid_: Purchase, transaction
15	
16	**Invoice**:
17	A request for payment sent to a customer after delivery.
18	_Avoid_: Bill, payment request
19	
20	**Customer**:
21	A person or organization that places orders.
22	_Avoid_: Client, buyer, account
23	```
24	
25	## Rules
26	
27	- **Be opinionated.** When multiple words exist for the same concept, pick the best one and list the others under `_Avoid_`.
28	- **Keep definitions tight.** One or two sentences max. Define what it IS, not what it does.
29	- **Only include terms specific to this project's context.** General programming concepts (timeouts, error types, utility patterns) don't belong even if the project uses them extensively. Before adding a term, ask: is this a concept unique to this context, or a general programming concept? Only the former belongs.
30	- **Group terms under subheadings** when natural clusters emerge. If all terms belong to a single cohesive area, a flat list is fine.
31	
32	## Single vs multi-context repos
33	
34	**Single context (most repos):** One `CONTEXT.md` at the repo root.
35	
36	**Multiple contexts:** A `CONTEXT-MAP.md` at the repo root lists the contexts, where they live, and how they relate to each other:
37	
38	```md
39	# Context Map
40	
41	## Contexts
42	
43	- [Ordering](./src/ordering/CONTEXT.md) — receives and tracks customer orders
44	- [Billing](./src/billing/CONTEXT.md) — generates invoices and processes payments
45	- [Fulfillment](./src/fulfillment/CONTEXT.md) — manages warehouse picking and shipping
46	
47	## Relationships
48	
49	- **Ordering → Fulfillment**: Ordering emits `OrderPlaced` events; Fulfillment consumes them to start picking
50	- **Fulfillment → Billing**: Fulfillment emits `ShipmentDispatched` events; Billing consumes them to generate invoices
51	- **Ordering ↔ Billing**: Shared types for `CustomerId` and `Money`
52	```
53	
54	The skill infers which structure applies:
55	
56	- If `CONTEXT-MAP.md` exists, read it to find contexts
57	- If only a root `CONTEXT.md` exists, single context
58	- If neither exists, create a root `CONTEXT.md` lazily when the first term is resolved
59	
60	When multiple contexts exist, infer which one the current topic relates to. If unclear, ask.
61
Write/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/CONTEXT.md
File created successfully at: /Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/CONTEXT.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
✳Réflexion

Glossary started at CONTEXT.md — Profile, Card, Feed, Like, Pass, Match are now pinned down.

Q3 — What exactly are the "pass" and "like" animations?

This is the one explicit engineering deliverable in the brief ("Add the pass and like animations"), so I don't want to guess it. You know the real app; I don't have it in front of me. Describe what actually happens in bpm when you tap the heart on a card, and when you tap the X.

Specifically what I need to know:

  1. Like — is there local feedback on the tapped element (heart pops/fills, burst particles?), and then what happens to the profile: does the whole view fly off (which direction?), fade, scale away?
  2. Pass — same question for the X: does the profile slide down, left, shrink+fade?
  3. Next profile entrance — does it slide in, fade in, or just appear scrolled back to top?

My recommendation if you'd rather leave it to my judgment (a liberty I'd flag in the README, as the brief allows):

  • Like: heart button does a spring pop and fills (white → surface color heart on primary yellow, say), a subtle burst; then the entire profile view translates up & off-screen with a slight scale-down + fade (~350ms, spring), next profile slides up into place from below with the scroll reset to top.
  • Pass: X spins/pops, profile view translates down & off with fade (opposite direction of like, so the two gestures feel semantically distinct), next profile enters the same way.
  • Both driven by Reanimated with worklets, buttons disabled during transition to avoid double-fire.

Do you want to describe the real animations, or take my version?

H
Hugo

pass animation is simple, a modal entering and disappearing on fade. The modal is full screen, during 1 second (without including the entering and exiting animations), a white cross (same as the button but bigger and centered) is shown.

like animation look like the same as the pass animation expect that there is the heart icon with primary color.

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Crystal clear — a full-screen interstitial overlay, not a card-level animation. Much simpler and very distinctive. Let me restate the spec as I'll build it, because there's one mechanism detail hiding in there:

Q4 — Confirm the overlay mechanics (one detail matters: what's behind it)

My reading of what you described:

  1. User taps heart on a card (or the X) → full-screen modal fades in (~200ms)
  2. Modal holds 1 second: solid background, centered icon — white cross for Pass, heart in primary #E1FF00 for Like
  3. While the overlay is opaque, the feed swaps to the next profile underneath (scroll reset to top)
  4. Modal fades out (~250ms), revealing the next profile

The load-bearing detail is the background: I'm assuming fully opaque surface color (#0D0D0D) so the profile swap is invisible and the fade-out is the "reveal" of the next profile. The alternative — a translucent scrim where you'd see the swap happen behind — would look broken, so opaque seems right.

Small things I'd fold in unless you object: a slight scale-pop on the icon as the overlay enters (pure fade with a static icon tends to feel dead), and both buttons disabled while the sequence runs so a double-tap can't skip two profiles.

My recommendation: yes to all of the above. Anything to correct — background color/opacity, icon size or motion, timings?

H
Hugo

yes to all of the above.

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Locked. The animation spec is fully resolved.

Q5 — Header controls: which of the three are functional?

The mockup header has, top-right: a pill with a filters icon and an undo/rewind icon, plus the circular blue boost button with a "1" badge. The brief's scope is "the feed + the two animations," so none of these strictly need to work. Options per control:

  • Filters — no meaningful behavior possible without backend filtering. Static icon.
  • Boost — same, it's a monetization feature. Static icon + badge.
  • Undo — this one is cheaply implementable and meaningful with our sequential feed: keep a history of consumed profiles, tapping undo restores the previous profile (and if it was a like, forgets that like). It's the kind of "relevant liberty" the brief invites, and it exercises the same state machinery the feed already needs.

My recommendation: filters and boost rendered faithfully but inert (no dead-looking disabled styling, just no-op), undo functional — flagged in the README as a deliberate liberty. If you'd rather keep the exercise lean, undo-as-static is also perfectly defensible; it's the mockup fidelity that matters most.

Functional undo, or everything static?

H
Hugo

Make it simple for these three buttons, redirect to a new page with a back button for each page (3 different pages). Make theses page simple, generate a really simple UI

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Noted — all three header buttons push their own simple placeholder page with a back button. I'll build them as three thin routes sharing one minimal placeholder layout (title in Cal Sans, back chevron, dark surface), so it stays DRY without inventing UI the brief doesn't ask for.

Q6 — Tab bar: same treatment? Plus two details the mockup forces

The mockup shows 4 tabs: Home (bpm logo icon, active), Likes (heart, yellow badge "4"), Matches (chat bubble), Profile (person).

  1. Scope — following the pattern you just set: Home = the feed, and Likes / Matches / Profile = the same style of dead-simple placeholder screens (no back button needed — they're tabs). Confirm?

  2. Likes badge "4" — I considered wiring it to the number of likes you send this session, but that would be semantically wrong: in a dating app the Likes tab is incoming likes, and we have no such data. So I recommend a static "4" exactly like the mockup, hardcoded at the tab-bar level.

  3. Home tab icon — it's the bpm logo, which I can't get from lucide. Do you have the logo as an SVG/PNG you can drop into src/assets/? If not, my recommendation: approximate it with a lucide icon of similar spirit (e.g. Activity/heart-pulse) and flag it in the README as a substitution.

Confirm 1 and 2, and tell me about 3?

H
Hugo

✳Réflexion

The SVG's there — single path with hardcoded fill="white", so I'll switch it to currentColor at setup time and tint it per tab state (white active, gray inactive) via react-native-svg. Trivial, just noting it.

Q7 — What happens when the feed runs out?

There are 10 profiles in the JSON. After the 10th like/pass, the feed is empty. Options:

A. Empty state + restart — a simple centered message ("Plus de profils pour le moment" / "No more profiles") with a button that resets the feed to the beginning. The restart is unrealistic for production but ideal for a test technique: the reviewer can replay the flow endlessly without killing the app.

B. Silent loop — after the last profile, start over from the first. Zero UI work, but it feels like a bug ("didn't I just see her?") and hides the fact that the feed is finite state you're managing correctly.

C. Pure empty state — honest, but the reviewer has to reload the app to try again.

My recommendation: A. It demonstrates end-of-data handling and keeps the demo replayable — flagged in the README as a demo affordance.

A, B, or C?

H
Hugo

A is perfect

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

The feed lifecycle is fully resolved. Now the card rendering cluster — one card type at a time. But first, a dependency that affects every card spec:

Q8 — UI language: English (like the mockup) or French (like the data)?

The mockup's chrome is English ("SESSIONS / WEEK", "Home", "Likes", "Matches", "Profile") but all the content in the JSON is French (prompt titles, answers, sport names, cities). The UI labels I'll need to invent — info card field labels, empty state text, locked card text, placeholder pages — have to pick a side.

A. English chrome — matches the mockup pixel-for-pixel ("code the exact bpm feed"), French content displayed as-is from data. Mixed languages, but the mockup itself already does this.

B. French chrome — coherent with the data and bpm's actual market, but diverges from the screenshot you gave me, and I'd be inventing every label's wording.

My recommendation: A. The mockup is the only UI source of truth I have, and "exact same UI" is the brief's core demand. Content stays French from the JSON; no i18n library either way — labels live in each feature's constants.ts.

A or B?

H
Hugo

Follow the data language

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

French it is — all chrome labels in French (e.g. "SÉANCES / SEMAINE", tabs "Accueil / Likes / Matchs / Profil", empty state "Plus de profils…"), living in constants.ts files, no i18n library. Content renders as-is from the JSON.

Now the cards, one type at a time, starting with the only one fully visible in the mockup:

Q9 — Picture card: does the prompt overlay render?

What the mockup shows and I'll reproduce directly: large rounded card, photo filling it (expo-image, cover), white circular heart button bottom-right. I'll use the thumbhash from the data as the loading placeholder — expo-image supports thumbhash natively, so photos fade in from a blurred preview for free. I'll also fix a constant aspect ratio (~3:4, matching the mockup) rather than honoring each image's natural size, so the feed rhythm stays stable.

The open question: PictureContent can carry promptKey/promptTitle — "a picture can optionally answer a prompt" (e.g. Inès' first photo answers "Mon endroit préféré"). The mockup shows no such overlay on the photo. Two readings:

A. Render it — when promptTitle is non-null, show a small pill overlaid on the photo (top-left, dark translucent background, white text). Data-faithful, Hinge-style; the field exists for a reason.

B. Ignore it — strictly mockup-faithful; the field goes unused on picture cards.

My recommendation: A — you just chose data over mockup on language, and silently dropping a modeled field feels wrong. Pill styling kept subtle so it doesn't fight the photo.

A or B? (And veto the fixed 3:4 ratio if the real app does something else.)

H
Hugo

Render it like the provide image.

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

That screenshot is gold — it answers Q9 and most of the sport card in one shot. Here's what I extracted:

Picture card (resolved): when promptTitle is non-null, the card becomes: dark panel, header row above the photo — rounded-square badge with a white quote glyph + prompt title in white — then the image below, rounded corners. When promptTitle is null: bare photo card like the first mockup. Heart stays bottom-right in the feed (this screenshot looks like a preview surface without reactions — your earlier "every card is likable" still stands).

It also confirms French chrome ("SÉANCES / SEMAINE") and gives me the sport card almost entirely. So let me lock that one down:

Q10 — Sport card: confirm my extracted spec (one invented part: ring sweep)

From the two screenshots combined:

  • Dark panel card. Right side: "SÉANCES / SEMAINE" in small gray uppercase, then one row per sport: name in white, dotted leader line, frequency value on the right.
  • Frequency mapping (from "Course à Pied 3-4", "Natation 1-2", "Trail 1-2" + first mockup's "5+"): little → "1-2", mid → "3-4", hard → "5+".
  • Positional accent colors — row 1 yellow (primary #E1FF00), row 2 purple, row 3 pink/magenta. Value text and its ring share the color. Purple + pink aren't in the brief's palette, so I'll add them to colors.ts (eyedropped ≈ #B36BFF / #FF2D87).
  • Left side: activity-rings graphic (custom react-native-svg) — one ring per sport (max 3 in the data), outermost ring = first sport, each on a dark muted track.
  • Two data fields go unused: sportIcon and the card-level overall trainingFrequency — neither appears in either screenshot. I'll leave them unrendered (flagged in README).

The one thing I'm inventing: ring sweep length ∝ frequency — little ≈ 33%, mid ≈ 66%, hard ≈ 100% of the circle. It matches how the screenshot's rings look (yellow fullest, pink shortest) and makes the graphic data-driven instead of decorative.

My recommendation: yes to all, including the sweep rule. Confirm / correct?

H
Hugo

Yes to all, including the sweep rule. I provided you another mockup with the card of type "info_card" and "prompt_answer"

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Excellent — this third mockup resolves the prompt card entirely and most of the info card. Extracted:

Prompt answer card (resolved): dark panel; header row = rounded-square badge (bpm heart-glyph icon) + prompt title in gray uppercase; answer text below in big white Cal Sans; heart button bottom-right. Generous padding. The category field (lifestyle/sport) isn't rendered anywhere — unused, flagged in README.

Also confirmed by this shot: French tabs exactly as proposed ("Accueil / Likes / Matchs / Profil"), heart button lives on cards in the feed, X is sticky bottom-left, and there's a "Bloquer & Signaler" pill next to it I'll ask about shortly.

Now the info card — it's visible but it opens the biggest data-vs-mockup gap yet:

Q11 — Info card: 4 fields or all 15?

The mockup's info card shows exactly: a stats row (🎂 50 │ 📏 170 cm │ 📍 Pignan, thin dividers), a separator, then a "looking for" section (🔍 + Endurance + gray tagline "Le jeu long, sans chrono" — which is clearly relationshipType: exclusive in athlete-speak).

But your InfoCardContent carries 15 fields — also job, education, originCity, zodiac, diet, drinking, smoking, drugs, kids, pets, religion. Options:

A. Mockup-strict: render exactly the 4 fields shown. The other 11 stay unused.

B. Mockup layout, extended (my recommendation): keep the exact stats row + relationship section, then extend the card with a wrapping chip grid for the remaining fields (lucide icon + French label each, e.g. 💼 Architecte, 🎓 ENSA, 🏠 Lyon, ♌ Lion, 🥗 Flexitarienne, 🍷 En soirée, 🚭 Jamais, 👶 En veut, 🐕 Chien, 🙏 Agnostique). You modeled those fields deliberately; Hinge-style vitals chips are the native pattern for them. Flagged as a liberty.

And a sub-question I can't dodge either way: relationshipType French labels. The screenshot gives exclusive → "Endurance — Le jeu long, sans chrono". Do you know the real app's wording for casual and intimate? If not, I'll invent in the same sporty register: casual → "Fractionné — À fond, puis on souffle", intimate → "Sprint — Intense, sans détour".

A or B, and real labels if you know them?

H
Hugo

Always show the age, height and localisation + separator + "looking for", keep the other info hidden for now. Keep the A option (mockup strict).

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Mockup-strict it is — the info card always shows the stats row (age / height / city) + separator + "looking for" section, nothing else. Since you didn't correct my invented labels, these stand unless you say otherwise: casual → "Fractionné — À fond, puis on souffle", intimate → "Sprint — Intense, sans détour" (matching the confirmed exclusive → "Endurance — Le jeu long, sans chrono").

Q12 — Four small fidelity calls (the two mockups disagree on some of these)

  1. "Bloquer & Signaler" pill — present in the real-app screenshot next to the X, absent from your first mockup. My rec: render it (it's part of the exact bottom bar) but inert — a fourth placeholder page for a block/report flow feels like scope creep. Include inert / include → placeholder page / skip entirely?

  2. Header name casing — first mockup says Inès, real screenshot says VANESSA. My rec: uppercase (textTransform: 'uppercase' on the header title only — data stays untouched).

  3. Prompt-badge icon — prompt cards show a bpm heart-face glyph; picture-with-prompt headers show a "99" quote glyph. I only have the bpm "b" logo SVG. Do you have the heart-face icon as an SVG? If not, my fallback: lucide Quote for picture headers, lucide MessageCircleHeart for prompt cards, flagged as substitution.

  4. Boost badge — "1" in mockup one, absent in the real screenshot. My rec: keep the badge "1" hardcoded, matching the first mockup like the Likes "4".

Answers on the four? (Even just "rec / rec / no asset / rec" works.)

Q13 — Locked picture card: what does "locked" look like?

The data is deliberately crippled (LockedPictureContent = just a thumbhash + optional promptTitle, "no storageKey or imageUrl on purpose") — it's a blurred teaser, presumably a premium hook in the real app. With no mockup, here's my proposed design:

  • Same card shell as a picture card (same ratio, same rounded corners), with the thumbhash rendered as the image itself — a thumbhash decodes to a soft blurry gradient, so the "blurred photo" effect comes straight from the data, no blur overlay needed.
  • Centered lock icon (lucide Lock) in a translucent dark circle — no invented marketing copy, no fake "Débloquer" paywall button (that whole flow is out of scope).
  • If promptTitle is non-null → same header treatment as picture-with-prompt (quote badge + title above the image).
  • Heart button stays bottom-right — per your rule, every card is likable, even one you can't see (which is actually a plausible premium mechanic: like blind, match reveals).

My recommendation: exactly that. The only variant worth considering is a one-line gray label under the lock ("Photo verrouillée") if you feel a bare icon is too cryptic.

Take it as proposed, or add the label?

H
Hugo

This is what the locked image looks like. One of the rules is that users can view the same number of photos from other profiles as they have provided themselves. Consider that the user had upload 3 pictures. This rule only applies to the pictures no other cards' type.

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

That mockup rewrites my proposal — and it smuggles in a brand-new business rule, so this needs careful grilling. What I extract visually (all straightforward, I'll build exactly this):

  • Blurred photo filling the card, lock icon in a translucent circle, bold white title "Tu vois autant de photos que tu en partages.", gray subtitle "Débloque plus de photos en ajoutant les tiennes.", and a white pill CTA "Ajouter mes photos" — which I'll render inert like "Bloquer & Signaler".
  • The heart bottom-right appears ghosted/dimmed on this card — more on that below.

Q14 — The photo-allowance rule: who applies it, and one glossary contradiction

a) Server-applied or client-applied? You said: a user sees as many photos per profile as they've uploaded themselves; our viewer has uploaded 3; pictures only. But look at the data: no profile has more than 2 unlocked picture cards — so with an allowance of 3, a client-side rule would never lock anything in this dataset. Two readings:

  • A. Server-driven: the locked_picture cards in the JSON are the rule's output (that's why they ship without imageUrl). The client just renders card types as received; "3 uploads" is context, not code.
  • B. Client-driven: the client enforces it too — a pure function takes a profile's cards + VIEWER_UPLOADED_PHOTOS_COUNT = 3 (constant) and demotes any picture card beyond the 3rd to locked rendering (we'd blur its real image). Never triggers on this dataset, but the logic exists, is unit-tested with vitest, and the constant is tweakable to demo it.

My recommendation: B. The rule as you phrased it is a viewer-side invariant, having it as a tested pure function is cheap, and it gives vitest something real to bite on. (A is fine if you consider the JSON already-resolved.)

b) Glossary contradiction to resolve: CONTEXT.md currently says "Every Card is likable, whatever its kind" — your words from Q2. But this mockup's heart is visibly dimmed on the locked card. Which wins: likable everywhere (heart normal on locked cards too), or locked cards excepted (heart shown but disabled — you can't like what you can't see)?

Chapter marked: "Card specs from mockups". Continue your current work.

a) A or B? b) Every card likable, or locked cards excepted?

H
Hugo

a) Go with option B. The best. b) it was a low fidelity wireframe, your question b) has no reason to exist. Here is the final rule: The user can "like" only what he can sees, every cards' type expect "info_card" is likable. "info_card" is just an info card, consider it like this, no like on this.

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Understood — the wireframe's ghost heart was noise, and the real rule is now sharp. Let me codify it in the glossary before moving on:

Glossary updated: Likable Card = unlocked picture, prompt answer, sport card — heart button on those three only. Info cards and locked pictures get no heart at all. And the Photo Allowance is now a named domain rule, implemented client-side as a pure function with VIEWER_UPLOADED_PHOTOS_COUNT = 3 in constants.ts, unit-tested.

The UI tree is now fully walked. Switching to the technical branch:

Q15 — Data layer: simulated API or direct import?

There's no backend — just feed-response-example.json. But your architecture template explicitly has api.ts # this feature's queries/mutations per feature, which implies TanStack Query. Two ways to honor that:

A. Simulated async API (my recommendation): a tiny lib/ client that resolves the JSON fixture after a short artificial delay (~500ms), consumed through TanStack Query in features/feed/api.ts (useFeed(), feedKeys). The JSON gets copied to a feature-local fixture file; typed as FeedResponse from your src/types/feed.ts — no zod re-validation, since duplicating those types into schemas is exactly what you forbade. What this buys: a real loading state (your thumbhash placeholders and a feed skeleton/spinner actually show), the api.ts seam a real app would have, and swapping in a real endpoint later means changing one function.

B. Direct synchronous import: import feed from './fixture.json' — zero ceremony, but api.ts becomes a lie, TanStack Query and loading states disappear, and the architecture doc's data-layer story has nothing behind it.

My recommendation: A, with one detail: the fixture lives at src/features/feed/fixtures/feed-response.json (the docs/ copy stays as the reference document, untouched).

A or B?

H
Hugo

Go with A, a tiny lib with articificial delay

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Data layer settled. Next branch: client state.

Q16 — Feed state: one zustand store, no persistence, no fake mutations?

Your architecture rule says "zustand slice only if genuinely client state" — and feed progression is exactly that (it's not server cache). Here's the shape I propose:

  • features/feed/store.ts → useFeedStore holding: current profile index, the set of liked card ids + passed profile ids (needed for the restart reset and honest domain modeling), and the transient reaction state ('like' | 'pass' | null) that drives the overlay → advance → reveal sequence. That makes the whole interaction a small, vitest-testable state machine — likeCard(cardId), passProfile(), advance(), restart().
  • No persistence — in-memory only; reloading the app restarts the feed. Fine for a test technique, and the restart button already covers replay.
  • No fake like/pass mutations in api.ts — a real app would POST likes, and I could theatrically model that with fake TanStack mutations, but fire-and-forget mutations that write to nowhere is indirection without value. api.ts keeps only the feed query; the README notes where mutations would slot in when a backend exists.

My recommendation: yes to all three. The one you might want differently is the last one — say so if you'd rather see the mutation seam demonstrated even against a fake backend.

Confirm?

H
Hugo

My architecture rule at the beginning of this conversation was the main architecture I used on every project, consider using only the part we really need to match the brief expectation as it is a technical test. To answer to Q16: yes to all three

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Good steer — the template is a menu, not a mandate. Here's the pruned structure, which also resolves the colors-vs-theme conflict:

Q17 — Sign off the minimal project tree

src/
  app/
    _layout.tsx            # providers (QueryClient, SafeArea), Cal Sans loading, splash
    (tabs)/
      _layout.tsx          # Accueil / Likes / Matchs / Profil
      index.tsx            # → FeedScreen
      likes.tsx  matches.tsx  profile.tsx      # → placeholders
    filters.tsx  rewind.tsx  boost.tsx         # → placeholders pushed from header
  features/
    feed/                  # the only feature
      components/          # picture-card, prompt-answer-card, sport-card, info-card,
                           # locked-picture-card, feed-header, reaction-overlay,
                           # like-button, feed-footer (X + Bloquer pill), empty-feed…
      api.ts               # useFeed + feedKeys (TanStack Query)
      store.ts             # useFeedStore (zustand)
      constants.ts         # FR labels, VIEWER_UPLOADED_PHOTOS_COUNT, frequency & relationship maps
      photo-allowance.ts   # the pure rule + photo-allowance.test.ts co-located
      fixtures/feed-response.json
      index.ts
  components/
    placeholder-screen.tsx # one shared screen, title prop — feeds all 6 placeholder routes
    ui/                    # only primitives genuinely shared: icon-button, badge
  lib/
    query-client.ts, mock-api.ts   # fetch-with-artificial-delay
  types/feed.ts            # YOUR file, untouched, imported everywhere via @/types/feed
  utils/colors.ts          # + utils/fonts.ts (Cal Sans family token)
  assets/

Dropped from the template because this test doesn't need them: (auth), theme/ (your explicit utils/colors.ts instruction wins; fonts get a sibling utils/fonts.ts), shared hooks/ (none exist), zod env parsing (no env vars), and schemas.ts anywhere (your no-duplication rule makes zod schemas for feed types illegal — types come from types/feed.ts, period).

Path alias @/* → src/*.

My recommendation: this tree as-is. Any file you'd move or a part you'd still cut?

H
Hugo

Perfect, go with this tree as-is.

✳Réflexion

Registry says: Expo SDK 57 (57.0.4) is the current latest, with React Native 0.86 — newer than what I'd have guessed, good thing I checked.

Q18 — Runtime & toolchain: SDK 57 + Expo Go + npm?

Three sub-decisions, all with a clear default:

  1. Expo SDK 57 / RN 0.86 — literally what you asked for ("latest stable"). It's freshly released (.0.4), but npx expo install pins ecosystem libs to SDK-compatible versions, so risk is low. Fallback to SDK 56 only if some dependency turns out broken.

  2. Expo Go, no dev build — the dependency list is deliberately Expo Go-safe: expo-router, expo-image (native thumbhash), expo-font, react-native-reanimated (overlay/pop animations), react-native-svg + transformer (bpm logo, sport rings), lucide-react-native, zustand, @tanstack/react-query. No custom native code → a reviewer runs npx expo start and scans the QR.

  3. npm as package manager — most frictionless for whoever reviews the test (no corepack/bun prerequisite). If you have a house preference (pnpm/bun/yarn), say it now, it costs nothing to adopt.

My recommendation: yes / yes / npm. Confirm or amend?

H
Hugo

yes / yes / npm

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Locked: SDK 57, Expo Go, npm.

Q19 — Vitest scope: pure logic only?

Vitest is mandated (alongside knip/biome/typecheck in the tooling issue), so the real question is what it bites on. React-native component rendering under vitest is friction city — the RN ecosystem's testing-library officially targets jest, so component tests would mean mocking half of react-native for marginal value on a UI this mockup-driven.

My recommendation — vitest tests pure TypeScript only, co-located per your naming rule:

  • photo-allowance.test.ts — the star: pictures beyond the viewer's allowance get demoted, locked_picture passes through, other card types never affected, boundary cases (exactly 3, zero uploads).
  • store.test.ts — the feed state machine: like advances and records the card id, pass advances, sequence ends in the empty state, restart resets everything, reaction state transitions.
  • Any pure mapper that grows actual logic (e.g. building the display model of a profile's cards). Static label Records in constants.ts don't get tests — testing data with data proves nothing.

No component/snapshot tests; visual fidelity is judged on the device, not in jsdom.

Agreed?

H
Hugo

I agree

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Then the design tree is fully walked — every branch resolved. Last question, about process:

Q20 — Deliverable process: issues now, implementation next?

Here's how I propose we close the loop, matching your workflow skills:

  1. git init + baseline commit first — the repo isn't versioned yet; the current state (docs, types, assets, CONTEXT.md) becomes the initial commit before any scaffolding.
  2. I break the plan into ordered local issues (via the to-issues flow → .grilled/issues/), tracer-bullet slices roughly like: ① Expo SDK 57 bootstrap (tree, alias, fonts, colors.ts) → ② tooling — knip/biome/typecheck/vitest, the independent issue you asked for → ③ navigation shell (tabs, header, placeholders, badges, logo) → ④ data layer (mock api, useFeed, loading state) → ⑤ picture card + vertical profile scroll → ⑥ prompt/sport/info cards (rings) → ⑦ locked picture + photo-allowance rule + tests → ⑧ like/pass store + reaction overlay animations + empty state/restart + tests → ⑨ README with the liberties list (restart affordance, invented casual/intimate labels, lucide substitutions, eyedropped purple/pink, ring-sweep rule, unused fields: sportIcon, overall trainingFrequency, prompt category, hidden info fields…).
  3. Then implementation — either you drive it issue-by-issue with /implement-next-issue, or I just proceed through them in order now.

My recommendation: yes to 1–2, and I proceed straight into implementation (3) in this session.

Green light — and do I keep rolling into the build once issues exist?

H
Hugo

yes to 1 and 2

✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Understood — I'll init the repo, commit the baseline, then generate the issues. Implementation stays parked until you kick it off (e.g. with /implement-next-issue).

Launching skill: to-issues
✳Réflexion

The skill wants your sign-off on the exact breakdown before I write the files. Here it is — 11 thin tracer-bullet slices, every one demoable on its own, all AFK (every decision was resolved during the grilling, so nothing needs a human in the loop):

# Title Type Blocked by
001 Expo SDK 57 bootstrap — create-expo-app, TS strict, @/ alias, approved tree, assets moved, utils/colors.ts + utils/fonts.ts, root layout with Cal Sans loading + splash. Demo: app boots in Expo Go, Cal Sans title renders AFK None
002 Tooling — knip, biome, typecheck, vitest + npm scripts (your independent issue) AFK 001
003 Navigation shell — 4 French tabs (bpm logo SVG, Likes badge "4"), feed header chrome (filters/rewind/boost + badge "1") pushing 3 placeholder pages, shared placeholder-screen AFK 001
004 Data layer — fixture, lib/mock-api + query-client, useFeed/feedKeys, loading state; header shows the current profile's firstname from data AFK 003
005 Picture card + profile card list — vertical scroll of cards by position, fixed-ratio photo card, thumbhash placeholder, quote-badge prompt header variant AFK 004
006 Prompt answer + info cards — gray-uppercase title / big Cal Sans answer; stats row + "looking for" section (invented casual/intimate labels) AFK 005
007 Sport card — SVG rings (sweep ∝ frequency, positional yellow/purple/pink), "SÉANCES / SEMAINE", dotted leaders, 1-2 / 3-4 / 5+ mapping AFK 005
008 Locked picture + Photo Allowance — locked card UI (blur, lock, copy, inert CTA), photo-allowance.ts pure function + tests, VIEWER_UPLOADED_PHOTOS_COUNT = 3 AFK 005
009 Feed store + like/pass interactions — useFeedStore state machine, hearts on Likable Cards only, sticky footer (X + inert "Bloquer & Signaler"), instant advance + scroll reset, empty state + restart, store tests AFK 006, 007, 008
010 Reaction overlay animations — the brief's core ask: opaque #0D0D0D interstitial, fade 200ms → hold 1s (white X / primary heart, icon pop) → fade 250ms, swap under overlay, buttons locked AFK 009
011 README + final polish — run instructions, liberties list, unused-fields note, all checks green AFK 002, 010

Quiz, per the skill: granularity OK (too coarse/fine anywhere)? Dependencies right? Any slice to merge/split — e.g. 006 bundles two simple cards deliberately, and 009/010 are split so the animation lands as its own reviewable slice? And do you agree everything is AFK?

H
Hugo

I agree with everything in AFK

✳
Claude

✳Réflexion

The grilling session is complete — every branch of the design tree got resolved, and the plan is now filed as issues.

What exists now:

  • CONTEXT.md — the domain glossary: Profile, Card, Feed, Like, Pass, Likable Card (unlocked pictures, prompts, sport cards — never info cards or locked pictures), Photo Allowance, Locked Picture, Match.
  • Git repo on main with two commits: the baseline (brief, types, assets, glossary) and the .grilled/ gitignore entry.
  • 11 issues in .grilled/issues/ (gitignored, all AFK, dependency-ordered): bootstrap → tooling → navigation shell → data layer → picture card → prompt/info cards → sport rings → locked picture + allowance → store + interactions → reaction overlay animations → README/polish. Each one is self-contained — all the specs we extracted from your mockups (French copy strings, color values, timings, mappings) are written into the issue bodies, since the screenshots themselves only exist in this conversation.

One liberty inside the issues to be aware of: issue 001 also asks for a CLAUDE.md capturing your naming/styling/composition rules — future implementation sessions will follow them automatically without needing this conversation. Strip it from the issue if you don't want that file.

Key decisions locked along the way: Expo SDK 57 / RN 0.86 / npm / Expo Go · profile-sequential vertical feed · opaque #0D0D0D interstitial (fade 200ms → hold 1s → fade 250ms, popping white X / yellow heart) · mockup-strict info card (4 fields) · frequency mapping 1-2 / 3-4 / 5+ with positional ring colors · client-side allowance rule with VIEWER_UPLOADED_PHOTOS_COUNT = 3 · vitest on pure logic only · utils/colors.ts wins over theme/.

Whenever you're ready, kick off the build with /implement-next-issue — it'll grab 001 (Expo bootstrap) first.

H
Hugo
/implement-next-issue
✳
Claude

The SDK 57 template already uses a src/ layout — convenient. Inspecting its config before merging:

⛭1 action · Bash
Bashcd /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-hugobayoud-prog-bpm-test-technique/de30dfa3-c8fb-4714-ab90-9b3e9b27fb3a/scratchpad/bpm-scaffold && cat package.json tsconfig.json app.json && echo "=== app dir ===" && find src -type f | sort && echo "=== global.css ===" && head -20 src/global.css
{
  "name": "bpm-scaffold",
  "main": "expo-router/entry",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "dependencies": {
    "@expo/ui": "~57.0.4",
    "expo": "~57.0.4",
    "expo-constants": "~57.0.3",
    "expo-device": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-font": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-glass-effect": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-image": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-linking": "~57.0.2",
    "expo-router": "~57.0.4",
    "expo-splash-screen": "~57.0.2",
    "expo-status-bar": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-symbols": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-system-ui": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-web-browser": "~57.0.0",
    "react": "19.2.3",
    "react-dom": "19.2.3",
    "react-native": "0.86.0",
    "react-native-gesture-handler": "~2.32.0",
    "react-native-reanimated": "4.5.0",
    "react-native-safe-area-context": "~5.7.0",
    "react-native-screens": "4.25.2",
    "react-native-web": "~0.21.0",
    "react-native-worklets": "0.10.0"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/react": "~19.2.2",
    "typescript": "~6.0.3"
  },
  "scripts": {
    "start": "expo start",
    "reset-project": "node ./scripts/reset-project.js",
    "android": "expo start --android",
    "ios": "expo start --ios",
    "web": "expo start --web",
    "lint": "expo lint"
  },
  "private": true
}
{
  "extends": "expo/tsconfig.base",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "paths": {
      "@/*": [
        "./src/*"
      ],
      "@/assets/*": [
        "./assets/*"
      ]
    }
  },
  "include": [
    "**/*.ts",
    "**/*.tsx",
    ".expo/types/**/*.ts",
    "expo-env.d.ts"
  ]
}
{
  "expo": {
    "name": "bpm-scaffold",
    "slug": "bpm-scaffold",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "orientation": "portrait",
    "icon": "./assets/images/icon.png",
    "scheme": "bpmscaffold",
    "userInterfaceStyle": "automatic",
    "ios": {
      "icon": "./assets/expo.icon"
    },
    "android": {
      "adaptiveIcon": {
        "backgroundColor": "#E6F4FE",
        "foregroundImage": "./assets/images/android-icon-foreground.png",
        "backgroundImage": "./assets/images/android-icon-background.png",
        "monochromeImage": "./assets/images/android-icon-monochrome.png"
      },
      "predictiveBackGestureEnabled": false
    },
    "web": {
      "output": "static",
      "favicon": "./assets/images/favicon.png"
    },
    "plugins": [
      "expo-router",
      [
        "expo-splash-screen",
        {
          "backgroundColor": "#208AEF",
          "image": "./assets/images/splash-icon.png",
          "imageWidth": 76
        }
      ]
    ],
    "experiments": {
      "typedRoutes": true,
      "reactCompiler": true
    }
  }
}

✳Réflexion

Template inspected. Checking the template's agent docs and root layout for the current SDK 57 idioms before writing our own:

⛭1 action · Bash
Bashcd /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-hugobayoud-prog-bpm-test-technique/de30dfa3-c8fb-4714-ab90-9b3e9b27fb3a/scratchpad/bpm-scaffold && cat src/app/_layout.tsx && echo "=== CLAUDE.md ===" && cat CLAUDE.md && echo "=== .claude/settings.json ===" && cat .claude/settings.json && echo "=== .gitignore ===" && cat .gitignore
import { DarkTheme, DefaultTheme, ThemeProvider } from 'expo-router';
import * as SplashScreen from 'expo-splash-screen';
import { useColorScheme } from 'react-native';

import { AnimatedSplashOverlay } from '@/components/animated-icon';
import AppTabs from '@/components/app-tabs';

SplashScreen.preventAutoHideAsync();

export default function TabLayout() {
  const colorScheme = useColorScheme();
  return (
    <ThemeProvider value={colorScheme === 'dark' ? DarkTheme : DefaultTheme}>
      <AnimatedSplashOverlay />
      <AppTabs />
    </ThemeProvider>
  );
}
=== CLAUDE.md ===
@AGENTS.md
=== .claude/settings.json ===
{
  "enabledPlugins": {
    "expo@claude-plugins-official": true
  }
}

✳Réflexion

Issue 001 — Expo SDK 57 bootstrap: implemented ✅ (on your expo-bootstrap branch)

What landed: - Expo SDK 57 / RN 0.86 / React 19.2, scaffolded from the official template but merged by hand — package.json pruned of demo dependencies (@expo/ui, expo-glass-effect, expo-symbols…), keeping what later issues need (reanimated 4.5, expo-image, gesture-handler). - tsconfig.json — strict, @/* → src/* (I dropped the template's second @/assets/* → root-assets alias: it would have shadowed src/assets imports). - app.json — name/slug/scheme bpm, forced dark UI, plain #0D0D0D splash; root assets/ holds only the app-icon files it references. - src/app/_layout.tsx — splash held until Cal Sans loads, light status bar, dark stack. index.tsx is the temporary proof screen (replaced in 003). - src/utils/colors.ts — full palette (brief + eyedropped accents/panel/muted); src/utils/fonts.ts — TITLE_FONT_FAMILY. - CLAUDE.md — your naming/styling/scope conventions for future sessions, plus .claude/launch.json so I can boot previews.

Checks: typecheck ✅ · Metro export bundles cleanly (font embedded) ✅ · booted the app and saw the Cal Sans title on the dark surface ✅ · src/types/feed.ts byte-identical ✅ · no hex outside colors.ts ✅. Biome/knip/vitest don't exist until issue 002 — nothing to run.

Noticed & adapted: you committed the issues into git, rewrote history, and branched expo-bootstrap between my turns — so I kept .grilled/ tracked (no re-ignoring) and the status flip to done is part of the diff.

Your turn: verify on-device with npm start + Expo Go, then commit when happy (I don't commit — use the commit skill). Remaining: 10 todo issues, none blocked or HITL; next up is 002 (tooling). Run /implement-next-issue again when ready.
